items.title: Turning Weekly Outages Into a Once-a-Year Event
items.subtitle: Root Cause Analysis & Proactive Hardening
items.situation: SanMar's VMware environment — 100+ hosts and 1,000+ VMs — was hit by P1 incidents on a roughly weekly basis, and for the first four years I was the sole VMware engineer covering it.
items.task: Bring the environment to a state stable enough that critical outages stopped being routine, without adding headcount.
items.action: Ran root cause analysis on every recurring incident pattern, hardened the infrastructure against the failure modes that kept resurfacing, and built out proactive monitoring through SolarWinds so problems surfaced before they became outages.
items.result: P1 incidents dropped from weekly to fewer than one per year — a stability shift that held for the rest of my eight years there.
items.metrics: P1 incidents: weekly → <1/year

items.title: Cutting Disaster Recovery Time From Hours to Minutes
items.subtitle: Site Recovery Manager Redesign
items.situation: Disaster recovery at SanMar relied on a process that, in a real failover, would have taken hours to bring critical systems back online.
items.task: Redesign the DR architecture to shrink that recovery window dramatically, without compromising data integrity.
items.action: Rebuilt disaster recovery around VMware Site Recovery Manager and vSphere Replication, replacing manual runbook steps with orchestrated, tested failover.
items.result: Recovery time objectives dropped from hours to minutes.
items.metrics: RTO: hours → minutes

items.title: Automating a Manual Annual Compliance Marathon
items.subtitle: PCI Reporting via PowerShell
items.situation: PCI compliance reporting was a manual, once-a-year push — time-consuming and error-prone given how much infrastructure it had to cover.
items.task: Turn an annual manual audit exercise into something repeatable and reliable.
items.action: Built a PowerShell automation workflow that generated PCI compliance reports directly from the infrastructure, replacing the manual process end to end.
items.result: A manual annual project became a repeatable, on-demand workflow — freeing up significant time and cutting the risk of human error in a compliance-critical process.
items.metrics: Manual annual audit → repeatable automated workflow

items.title: Migrating Live E-Commerce Storage With Zero Downtime
items.subtitle: Dell EMC VNX-E → Pure Storage
items.situation: SanMar needed to move its e-commerce platform's storage off an aging Dell EMC VNX-E array onto Pure Storage — with the platform staying live and revenue-generating the entire time.
items.task: Execute a full storage platform migration for a business-critical, always-on system with no acceptable downtime window.
items.action: Coordinated cross-team cutover planning with storage, networking, and application teams, sequencing the migration to keep the platform online throughout.
items.result: Completed the migration with zero downtime and a seamless cutover for the live e-commerce platform.
items.metrics: Zero downtime on a live revenue-generating platform

items.title: Deploying Resilient Edge Infrastructure Across Nine States
items.subtitle: VxRail & vSAN HCI Rollout
items.situation: SanMar operated nine physical warehouses that needed modern, resilient compute and storage without a large on-site IT footprint at each location.
items.task: Architect and deploy hyperconverged infrastructure that could run reliably and independently across nine geographically distributed sites.
items.action: Architected and deployed VxRail and vSAN HCI nodes at all nine warehouses, coordinating directly with Dell and internal networking teams to keep the rollout scalable and reliable at the edge.
items.result: Delivered resilient, distributed infrastructure supporting warehouse operations company-wide, on a consistent architecture that simplified ongoing support.
items.metrics: 9 warehouses standardized on distributed HCI infrastructure

items.title: Building an Internal Pentesting Playground From Scratch
items.subtitle: Security Awareness Hackathon
items.situation: Security awareness across engineering teams was inconsistent, with no hands-on way for people to learn how systems actually get compromised.
items.task: Create a low-stakes way for engineers across teams to practice offensive security skills and build shared security awareness.
items.action: Established and led an internal pentesting playground paired with a hackathon event, designed to promote security awareness and cross-team collaboration.
items.result: Grew engineering teams a recurring, hands-on way to build security skills together, strengthening the org's security culture beyond compliance checklists.
items.metrics: Recurring cross-team security hackathon established

items.title: Cutting Manual Validation Time by 40% Through Automated Health Checks
items.subtitle: Practice Architect, GCSIT
items.situation: Infrastructure health checks and reporting for healthcare and public-sector clients were done manually, consuming significant engineer time on every engagement.
items.task: Reduce the manual burden of infrastructure validation without losing accuracy or coverage.
items.action: Automated infrastructure health checks and reporting using PowerShell, building it into the standard delivery process across client engagements.
items.result: Cut manual validation time by approximately 40%, freeing up time for higher-value delivery work and training junior engineers.
items.metrics: Manual validation time −40%

items.title: Eliminating Snapshot Sprawl via Automated Cleanup Reports
items.subtitle: PowerCLI Snapshot Lifecycle Management
items.situation: With broad access to create snapshots across the SanMar environment, unmanaged snapshot sprawl began degrading storage capacity and virtual machine performance[cite: 3].
items.task: Track, manage, and systematically clean up stale snapshots to reclaim capacity and protect environment performance[cite: 3].
items.action: Partnered with a Windows administrator to build a PowerCLI and PowerShell script that compiled detailed snapshot histories (creators, dates, and notes) into an HTML report emailed daily at 7 AM[cite: 3]. Followed up with users to validate removals, then expanded the logic to automatically purge non-exempt snapshots older than 48 hours[cite: 3].
items.result: Reclaimed substantial storage capacity, improved virtual machine disk performance, and eliminated the need for routine manual audits[cite: 3].
items.metrics: Automated lifecycle enforcement for all infrastructure snapshots[cite: 3]

items.title: Fixing SAN Multi-Pathing Bottlenecks at Scale
items.subtitle: PowerCLI Round-Robin Automation
items.situation: The VMware environment was routing SAN traffic through only a single active path out of multiple available lanes, severely bottlenecking data transmission across a massive host footprint[cite: 3].
items.task: Reconfigure all hosts and LUNs to utilize a round-robin policy without undertaking months of slow, manual host-by-host adjustments[cite: 3].
items.action: Validated the round-robin policy via ESXi CLI on a single test LUN[cite: 3]. Realizing manual execution across the cluster was unsustainable, researched and built a comprehensive PowerCLI script to automate the multipathing policy updates across all production hosts simultaneously during a safe evening maintenance window[cite: 3].
items.result: Compressed an estimated two-month manual configuration project into a single evening, evenly distributing storage traffic across available paths with zero production impact[cite: 3].
items.metrics: Reduced a 2-month manual timeline to a single evening deployment[cite: 3]

items.title: Evacuating On-Premises Blogs under Tight M&A Deadlines
items.subtitle: Cloud Platform Transition
items.situation: Following the Broadcom acquisition, we faced a hard deadline to evacuate our physical data center, but the cloud migration of our public technical blogs platform was stalling due to low vendor engagement[cite: 3].
items.task: Take ownership of the migration stream, realign the third-party vendor, and move the platform to the cloud before the deadline[cite: 3].
items.action: Instituted weekly operational syncs with the vendor account team to enforce strict SLA metrics, audited contract scopes, and built a parallel cloud test environment to run validations[cite: 3]. Spearheaded technical training sessions for internal blog administrators to ensure Day-2 operational readiness[cite: 3].
items.result: Migrated the entire blogs platform to the cloud within the acquisition deadline with zero public downtime or interruption[cite: 3].
items.metrics: 100% cloud migration completed on schedule with zero user-facing downtime[cite: 3]

items.title: Stabilizing High-Risk Maintenance Calls
items.subtitle: Technical Risk Mitigation & Peer Support
items.situation: A highly visible, high-risk infrastructure maintenance task needed to be executed, and a colleague prone to panic under pressure was scheduled to lead it[cite: 3].
items.task: Ensure the success of the maintenance window and protect team standing while supporting the colleague's professional growth[cite: 3].
items.action: Intervened early with a calm, structured compromise: kept the colleague as the named lead but remained on the bridge throughout the task to handle back-end complications, manage anxiety, and step in if technical roadblocks occurred[cite: 3].
items.result: Completed the high-risk maintenance without a single incident, successfully building peer confidence and reinforcing trust with business stakeholders[cite: 3].
items.metrics: Zero incident rate on highly visible maintenance windows[cite: 3]

items.title: Standing Up the Company’s First AWS Environment
items.subtitle: Rapid Self-Taught Cloud Architecture
items.situation: SanMar lacked an official public cloud footprint, and I was tasked on a Friday afternoon with learning AWS from scratch and designing a deployment framework by Monday[cite: 3].
items.task: Architect, test, and hand over a functional cloud landing zone within a strict two-week window without internal mentors or external vendor support[cite: 3].
items.action: Spent the weekend dissecting AWS documentation on VPC structures and EC2 design[cite: 3]. Orchestrated a cross-functional whiteboard session on Monday with networking and OS admins to map out components, and integrated a dedicated EC2 adapter to convert on-premises VMware instances into cloud workloads[cite: 3].
items.result: Successfully stood up and validated the production AWS environment within the two-week timeline before handing operations over to the core systems teams[cite: 3].
items.metrics: Zero-to-production cloud environment in 14 days[cite: 3]

items.title: Integrating Container Orchestration into Enterprise vSphere
items.subtitle: Pivoting from VIC to DCoS Mesos for Developer Velocity
items.situation: Developers were running containerized code locally on individual laptops and requested separate remote desktops, missing a unified, scalable development platform[cite: 3].
items.task: Build a centralized, cost-effective container environment by leveraging our existing vSphere virtualization cluster[cite: 3].
items.action: Collaborated with development and DevOps leads to pilot VMware Integrated Containers (VIC) along with a Harbor image registry[cite: 3]. When validation revealed strict performance limits with VIC, pivoted the architecture to run DCoS Mesos on optimized Linux nodes inside vSphere to host the apps[cite: 3].
items.result: Delivered a rapid container-hosting platform that cut capital expenditure on physical desktop gear and secured an invite to early AWS EKS architectural boards[cite: 3].
items.metrics: Maximized existing cluster hardware to eliminate dedicated developer hardware costs[cite: 3]

items.title: Resolving a Single Point of Failure at a Core Logistics Hub
items.subtitle: Emergency Warehouse Outage Remediation
items.situation: A critical regional distribution warehouse suffered severe operational blockages due to unstable underlying infrastructure tied to a single point of failure[cite: 3].
items.task: Restore immediate operational stability over the weekend and engineer a permanent high-availability architecture[cite: 3].
items.action: Commanded a comprehensive weekend turnaround effort involving controlled system reboots and isolated validation passes[cite: 3]. Compiled a high-availability proposal for executive management to replace the legacy hardware with an explicitly redundant alternative[cite: 3].
items.result: Returned the distribution center to full capacity by Monday morning and secured executive sign-off for a complete redundancy upgrade[cite: 3].
items.metrics: Zero Monday morning operational downtime achieved post-outage[cite: 3]

items.title: Architecting a Tiered Log Retention System for PCI Compliance
items.subtitle: Cost-Effective Logging with Graylog & Log Insight
items.situation: Strict PCI compliance audits required long-term security log preservation, but standard enterprise tooling alternatives like Splunk or Datadog were cost-prohibitive[cite: 3].
items.task: Design a highly resilient, multi-tiered retention system that meets audit compliance constraints without increasing licensing spend[cite: 3].
items.action: Built a tiered storage workflow using VMware vRealize Log Insight for fast, short-term troubleshooting access[cite: 3]. Configured programmatic log shipping pipelines to route historical audit logs down to an open-source Graylog server cluster sitting on lower-tier disk space[cite: 3].
items.result: Passed all PCI compliance logging requirements seamlessly while avoiding expensive third-party ingestion and software seat licenses[cite: 3].
items.metrics: Achieved 100% compliance log retention with zero additional software overhead[cite: 3]

items.title: Rebuilding Fabric Infrastructure After a Critical Switch Outage
items.subtitle: Emergency Scripted SAN Recovery
items.situation: During a complex data center SAN upgrade involving live cable migrations, an active production switch was inadvertently disconnected, causing storage paths to drop instantly[cite: 3].
items.task: Arrest the resulting cluster panic, restore data paths safely, and systematically bring production systems back online[cite: 3].
items.action: Took immediate command of the bridge, directing application admins to shut down affected systems cleanly to protect data integrity[cite: 3]. Wrote and deployed an automated PowerShell script to sequentially spin up VMs based on application tier dependencies once physical fabric connectivity was restored[cite: 3].
items.result: Contained the downtime to an off-hours window with zero client impact, prompting a full data center cable remediation and an organization-wide push for disaster recovery testing[cite: 3].
items.metrics: Restored complex multi-tier application dependency stack with zero data corruption[cite: 3]

items.title: Systematizing Cluster Diagnostics to Restore Platform Trust
items.subtitle: Standardizing Root-Cause Routines with Jira & Confluence
items.situation: The virtualization cluster suffered from persistent mid-week P1 incidents that occurred post-change windows, leading surrounding teams to blame the underlying VMware platform[cite: 3].
items.task: Isolate the actual cross-stack environmental triggers causing the instability and establish a transparent tracking process[cite: 3].
items.action: Partnered with VMware, Cisco, and storage engineering vendors to map out hidden configuration conflicts[cite: 3]. Structured a systematic tracking methodology using Jira parent/subtask relations and stood up a central Confluence knowledge repository to share proven configurations across the engineering organization[cite: 3].
items.result: Drove down P1 incident rates drastically, established a template-driven incident investigation workflow, and restored confidence in core infrastructure[cite: 3].
items.metrics: Restored organizational trust in core virtual infrastructure[cite: 3]

items.title: The VirtualBox Sandbox Pivot That Launched a Career
items.subtitle: Resolving a Legacy OS Compatibility Blockade
items.situation: While working at the Service Desk, a mandatory company-wide 64-bit Windows upgrade stalled because two critical teams relied on an unmaintained legacy application with zero remaining documentation[cite: 3].
items.task: Unblock the corporate operating system deployment without breaking day-to-day operations for the affected business units[cite: 3].
items.action: Embedded with the target users to map out application dependencies[cite: 3]. Researched local hypervisors, built a custom 32-bit virtual machine sandbox using VirtualBox on top of the new 64-bit host, and engineered a reliable host-to-guest data transfer mechanism to keep workflows seamless[cite: 3].
items.result: Unblocked the global compliance upgrade, eliminated vendor replacement searches, and directly secured an promotion into the virtualization engineering group[cite: 3].
items.metrics: 100% legacy user migration completed with zero workflow disruption[cite: 3]

items.title: Building Team Redundancy Through Structured Mentorship
items.subtitle: Cross-Training Engineering Partners
items.situation: The virtualization engineering team lacked deep operational redundancy, making operations highly dependent on a single point of failure for on-call escalations[cite: 3].
items.task: Up-level a skilled Windows administrator into a confident, competent backup engineering resource for virtual infrastructure[cite: 3].
items.action: Built a structured curriculum anchored directly to the VMware associate-level exam objectives[cite: 3]. Conducted focused whiteboard and screen-share sessions three times a week, breaking down conceptual storage, networking, and compute virtualization abstractions[cite: 3].
items.result: The mentee successfully passed their VMware certification exam, establishing vital operational redundancy and providing dependable coverage during emergencies[cite: 3].
items.metrics: Expanded team backup capabilities through formalized peer mentorship[cite: 3]

items.title: Internalizing the Enterprise Upgrade Pipeline
items.subtitle: Eliminating Professional Services Dependencies
items.situation: SanMar traditionally outsourced all high-visibility cluster maintenance and major version vSphere upgrades to third-party professional services contractors, inflating consulting spend and capping internal skill development[cite: 3].
items.task: Build the necessary internal capabilities to execute complex upgrades in-house, ensuring complete operational autonomy[cite: 3].
items.action: Constructed fully isolated laboratory environments to stress-test upgrade paths and mock potential failures[cite: 3]. Drafted exhaustive, internal implementation runbooks and hosted walkthrough sessions to prepare business stakeholders and engineering teams[cite: 3].
items.result: Successfully brought the entire enterprise upgrade cycle in-house, eliminating external vendor dependency and establishing a repeatable upgrade framework[cite: 3].
items.metrics: Reduced external contractor reliance to 0% for core upgrade cycles[cite: 3]

items.title: Filling 1,000 Seats in 48 Hours
items.subtitle: VMware Cloud Foundation Exam Prep Series
items.situation: The VMware community wanted structured help preparing for the new VCF certification, but no program existed for it yet[cite: 1].
items.task: Launch a VCF Administrator exam prep series from scratch and get it in front of a global community fast[cite: 1].
items.action: Designed and launched the series, then used real-time community feedback and engagement data to adapt session topics, schedules, and formats as it grew[cite: 1].
items.result: Signups passed 1,000 within 48 hours of launch[cite: 1].
items.metrics: 1,000+ signups in 48 hours[cite: 1]

items.title: Reviving a Chapter From One Meeting a Year to Seven
items.subtitle: Seattle VMUG Turnaround
items.situation: The Seattle VMUG chapter I inherited was largely dormant — one meeting a year, minimal engagement[cite: 1].
items.task: Rebuild the chapter into something people actually showed up for, in-person and virtually[cite: 1].
items.action: Designed programming that combined technical education with genuine community building, recruited new leadership, and built vendor partnerships to sustain it[cite: 1].
items.result: Grew the chapter from one annual meeting to seven events a year within the first year — a turnaround that led to my nomination as U.S. West Regional VMUG Champion[cite: 1].
items.metrics: 1 event/year → 7 events/year[cite: 1]

items.title: Holding a Global Community Together Through an Acquisition
items.subtitle: Broadcom Transition
items.situation: The Broadcom acquisition of VMware put a 23,000+ member community's platforms and continuity at risk, with contributor trust on the line during the transition[cite: 1].
items.task: Keep the community's core platforms running and contributor trust intact through the merger[cite: 1].
items.action: Served as a key escalation point for platform issues during the transition, maintaining operations across WordPress, Slack, and Discord while aligning advocacy programs to the new organization[cite: 1].
items.result: Maintained platform continuity and contributor trust through one of the most disruptive periods in the community's history[cite: 1].
items.metrics: 23,000+ members retained through the transition[cite: 1]

items.title: Scaling Mentorship Across the Western United States
items.subtitle: Regional VMUG Champion Program
items.situation: Local VMUG chapters across the western US varied widely in health — some thriving, many dormant or underperforming[cite: 1].
items.task: Raise the baseline quality and consistency of chapter leadership across an entire region[cite: 1].
items.action: Mentored 60+ local VMUG leaders directly, built reusable community playbooks and onboarding templates, and ran needs assessments to reactivate underperforming chapters[cite: 1].
items.result: Elevated event quality and participation region-wide, and built the templates that let new leaders launch chapters faster than before[cite: 1].
items.metrics: 60+ leaders mentored across the western US[cite: 1]

items.title: Advancing Diversity and Accessibility Across a Global User Group
items.subtitle: VMUG Diversity & Inclusion Councilmember
items.situation: VMware User Groups' speaker rosters, event planning, and outreach didn't consistently reflect or reach underrepresented technologists[cite: 1].
items.task: Push concrete improvements to representation, accessibility, and inclusion across VMUG's programming[cite: 1].
items.action: Served on the Diversity & Inclusion Council, advising on inclusive programming and speaker recruitment practices, and built mentorship and outreach programs to create safe spaces for underrepresented voices[cite: 1].
items.result: Helped shift VMUG's programming and outreach practices toward measurably more inclusive speaker recruitment and community spaces[cite: 1].
items.metrics: 2 years on the D&I Council shaping org-wide programming practices[cite: 1]

items.title: Keeping 800 Time-Sensitive Requests Moving Under Strict SLAs
items.subtitle: Health Services Consultant, WA State DOH
items.situation: A high-volume public-sector environment processes roughly 800 daily licensing, renewal, and account workflows, each bound by strict SLA expectations[cite: 1].
items.task: Keep that volume moving accurately and on time, while reducing repeat questions and account defects reaching the team[cite: 1].
items.action: Guided callers through licensing and account issues with clear, consistent communication, investigated account defects and data inconsistencies to give internal teams clear technical context, and built documentation and response templates to standardize common steps[cite: 1].
items.result: Contributed to a more standardized, lower-friction workflow in a high-volume environment, with documentation now reused across the team to cut repeat questions[cite: 1].
items.metrics: ~800 daily workflows supported under strict SLA[cite: 1]

items.title: Boosting Speaker Submissions for VMware Explore
items.subtitle: Shifting from broad blasts to direct outreach for the Code Theater
items.situation: I was managing the VMware Code program, which included running the community theater at the VMware Explore conference. We needed community members to submit session proposals, but initial engagement was low and we were struggling to attract a solid lineup of speakers.
items.task: My goal was to drive up submission numbers and land high-quality presenters. I needed to find out what topics actually interested the community and connect with the right people to get them on stage.
items.action: I dug into past conference data to see what worked before. I checked YouTube replay metrics, LinkedIn hashtags, and tracking tools like TalkWalker to pinpoint the most popular sessions and speakers from the last two years. Armed with that list, I dropped the generic community-wide emails and started messaging people directly on LinkedIn, Slack, and Discord. I reached out to established speakers and invited new, promising voices to pitch a session.
items.result: The personal approach worked. Shifting to targeted, one-on-one outreach significantly increased our session submissions and built stronger individual relationships with our presenters. The community theater ended up with a great mix of trusted experts and fresh perspectives.
items.metrics: Increased theater session submissions through 1-on-1 outreach channels.

items.title: Advocating for the Right Community Platform
items.subtitle: Moving VMware {code} from Slack to Discord instead of internal defaults
items.situation: In early 2024, we found out our VMware {code} Slack channel was getting shut down. Internal leadership wanted to push everyone to Google Spaces because it was already approved for internal company use. The team was ready to just roll with it to save time.
items.task: As the Community Program Manager, I had to protect our user engagement. Google Spaces worked fine for internal employees, but it wasn't the right fit for an external developer audience.
items.action: I paused the migration and talked to our members. They told me Google Spaces felt clunky, was tough for outsiders to access, and lacked the chat features they used daily. I took that feedback, researched alternatives, and built a data-backed case for Discord. Even with internal pressure to use the standard corporate tool, I stood my ground to protect the user experience.
items.result: Leadership bought into the plan, and we moved to Discord instead. The transition was incredibly smooth, and the community was thrilled that we actually listened to them. Active participation stayed high because we picked a tool that developers actually wanted to use.
items.metrics: Maintained 100% community continuity during platform transition.

items.title: Scrappy Livestreaming on a $1,000 Budget
items.subtitle: Bringing back session recordings for global VMware Explore members
items.situation: In 2023, budget cuts meant the VMware {code} and Community Theater sessions at VMware Explore weren't recorded. Our global members who couldn't travel due to costs or visa issues were highly disappointed. They felt left out of the technical conversations.
items.task: I wanted to bring back video coverage for the 2024 events. My director gave me the green light, but only if I could pull off equipment, software, and staffing for a grand total of $1,000.
items.action: I went to Best Buy to piece together a low-cost camera and cabling setup that could run a live feed through StreamYard. I mapped out the entire workflow and color-coded all the gear so it was foolproof. Since I couldn't afford a production crew, I ran the cameras for the main theater myself and recruited a team of community volunteers to help cover the rest of the rooms after training them on the setup. We promoted the streams on LinkedIn and Twitter and archived everything on YouTube.
items.result: We recorded and live-streamed more than 60 sessions, pulling in thousands of views. Global members got their access back, and speakers who boycotted the previous year returned. The project restored deep community trust and proved we could scale our content globally without a massive budget. The main Explore events team noticed the success in Las Vegas and Barcelona and offered to handle our recording budget for 2025.
items.metrics: 60+ sessions streamed, 7,700+ views, $1,000 total spend.

items.title: Aligning Community Content with Business Priorities
items.subtitle: Kicking off the year with a focused Code Coaches initiative
items.situation: At the start of the year, I needed to get our top community leaders—the Code Coaches—aligned on a plan. The challenge was finding the sweet spot between what our members wanted to learn and what the company needed to promote.
items.task: I needed to design a content roadmap that kept community engagement high while supporting a major corporate push around VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF).
items.action: I brought the Code Coaches together for an open feedback session to see what topics were trending on the ground. A major takeaway was that while we had plenty of deep technical experts, we were lacking foundational, back-to-basics content for beginners. I took that insight and mapped it to our business goals. I reached out to internal product teams, technical writers, and engineers, getting them to collaborate with our coaches to build introductory guides and tutorials centered on automation and VCF.
items.result: We launched a highly successful content campaign that balanced deep technical deep-dives with clear, foundational tutorials. Members got the practical learning resources they asked for, and internal product teams gained valuable visibility by contributing directly to the community.
items.metrics: Launched cross-functional content campaigns matching user demands to product goals.

items.title: Using Sprint Campaigns to Drive Member Growth
items.subtitle: Tracking technical content engagement with Bitly and Sprout Social
items.situation: We frequently used LinkedIn and Twitter to keep our audience active, but during quieter periods, engagement would drop off. We needed a clearer understanding of what specific posts actually cut through the noise.
items.task: My task was to build short social media campaigns, track user behavior with precise metrics, and quickly pivot our approach based on real-time data.
items.action: I set up rapid two-week test sprints. One sprint might focus purely on deep technical tutorials, while the next would spotlight individual community members. To see exactly where people clicked, I generated unique Bitly short links for different platforms and paired that data with engagement metrics from Sprout Social to monitor comments, shares, and overall reach.
items.result: The numbers gave us a clear answer: technical problem-solving content and community spotlights outpaced everything else. By leaning heavily into those formats and pairing them with clear calls-to-action, we drove consistent community participation and maintained a steady influx of new members.
items.metrics: Used 2-week data sprints to pinpoint and scale top-performing content types.

items.title: Scaling Tech Study Groups Internationally
items.subtitle: Using data from English pilots to map out Spanish and Japanese launches
items.situation: We ran a highly successful English-language study group for the VCP-VCF Administrator exam. Because it did so well, I wanted to expand the program to our Spanish and Japanese-speaking members so they weren't left out of global training initiatives.
items.task: I needed to design an expansion strategy for these two distinct regions, using the operational data from our English group to guarantee a successful rollout.
items.action: I broke down the metrics from the first pilot—analyzing Zoom registrations, Discord chat activity, and advocacy tool metrics to see exactly how people discovered and interacted with the sessions. The data showed that Zoom worked best for attendance, Discord kept daily conversations alive, and LinkedIn drove the most sign-ups. I replicated this exact tech stack for the new regions. I also coordinated with internal teams to track down translated study guides, adjusting our launch timeline to match when the pending Spanish documentation would be officially ready.
items.result: Using a data-backed blueprint allowed us to plan the international expansion with high confidence. By choosing the exact platforms our users preferred and aligning the schedule with localized study materials, we set up the upcoming Spanish and Japanese groups for immediate traction.
items.metrics: Mapped out global expansion across 3 platforms using pilot performance data.

items.title: Managing a Broken Developer Portal Post-Acquisition
items.subtitle: Stepping into an unguided migration and creating temporary documentation
items.situation: Right after Broadcom acquired VMware, I inherited ownership of the developer portal, which hosted our public SDKs and APIs. The previous owner was laid off before we could do a handoff, leaving me with zero onboarding. To make things harder, the entire site was in the middle of a chaotic migration to Broadcom's back-end infrastructure.
items.task: I had to quickly audit the migration, keep the platform usable for developers, and handle community frustration during a major corporate transition.
items.action: I tracked down the IT project manager running the backend migration to figure out our timelines and started testing the live environments. When the site went live, it was hit with broken links and failed redirects. I monitored our Discord and Slack channels to collect bug reports from users in real time. Since the company hadn't released an official update, I compiled a comprehensive workaround guide with working links, turned it into a PDF, and shared it across our community channels.
items.result: The community highly appreciated the transparency. The temporary documentation kept developers moving and bridged a major communication gap while I worked behind the scenes with the engineering team to permanently fix the broken links, successfully protecting user trust during a chaotic corporate shift.
items.metrics: Maintained community developer trust during a major infrastructure migration.

items.title: Taking Over Global Forum Operations on Khoros
items.subtitle: Stepping in during a maternity leave to keep global technical forums staffed
items.situation: While managing my usual community programs, a coworker went on maternity leave. I was asked to step up and take over the management of the entire global VMware forum platform on Khoros, which was outside my original job scope.
items.task: I had to keep our large, highly active user forums running smoothly, coordinate with a global team of volunteer moderators, and ensure technical issues were addressed without any dip in response times.
items.action: I jumped directly into the private backend Slack channels where our moderators hang out. Because I already had good relationships with them and shared an engineering background, it was easy to get on the same page. I partnered with product managers to spot gaps in forum coverage, making sure every product space had active eyes on it. When new corporate initiatives launched, I wrote custom HTML to quickly roll out dedicated forum spaces to support them.
items.result: By the time my colleague returned, the forums had experienced zero downtime or moderation backlogs. Active communication kept the volunteer team motivated, and our technical communities remained fully supported throughout the transition.
items.metrics: Managed 100% forum continuity with zero coverage gaps over a multi-month transition.

items.title: Maintaining Momentum Without Swag or Speakers
items.subtitle: Launching 'vCoffee' sessions to focus on grassroots discussions
items.situation: There were periods where corporate resources were extremely tight. We found ourselves trying to run VMUG and VMware Code events with no budget for promotional giveaways, no company swag, and no available corporate speakers to pitch technical slide decks.
items.task: I had to keep our regional chapters active and make sure members still wanted to show up to meetings, even without the usual incentives like free gear or corporate presentations.
items.action: I changed the playbook entirely. Instead of trying to hype up a standard corporate presentation, I launched informal, low-pressure feedback meetups called "vCoffee" sessions. I was completely honest with the community about our lack of resources, but re-centered the events entirely around open discussion, peer-to-peer troubleshooting, and networking. The focus shifted from listening to a speaker to talking with each other.
items.result: Attendance stayed remarkably steady. Turning the lack of corporate resources into an opening for genuine conversation actually strengthened community ties. Members appreciated the transparency, and the interactive format proved that people value peer connections far more than free t-shirts.
items.metrics: Sustained meeting attendance rates purely through peer-to-peer formats.

items.title: Bypassing a Broken SDK Download System
items.subtitle: Creating a temporary cloud workaround during a multi-week outage
items.situation: While monitoring our developer forums, I noticed a sudden wave of complaints about our SDKs and APIs. A system glitch had accidentally locked down access to our public, free downloads, restricting them to a tiny group of internal users.
items.task: The technical fix on the backend was going to take our engineering teams several weeks to deploy. I needed an immediate workaround so developers worldwide wouldn't be blocked from building on our platform.
items.action: I worked with internal teams to verify the bug, then used my own internal access permissions to download the necessary SDK files. I packaged them into a secure, shared Box.com repository and posted the link directly in the active forum threads. I gave the community weekly progress updates on the official fix and personally helped users who were hitting account registration walls.
items.result: The temporary cloud share kept our global developer base moving without a single day of total project disruption. I safely pulled down the Box share once the engineering team rolled out the permanent system fix, saving weeks of downtime and earning a lot of goodwill from our users.
items.metrics: Eliminated weeks of developer downtime by deploying an immediate alternative file share.

items.title: Pushing for Video Accessibility at VMUG Connect
items.subtitle: Using past community friction to change leadership's mind on recording events
items.situation: While planning the VMUG Connect event, I kept thinking about how upset our members were when we didn't record the sessions at VMware Explore. Our community hated missing out on technical content just because they couldn't afford to travel to an in-person event.
items.task: I wanted to ensure we didn't repeat that mistake. My goal was to convince leadership to record VMUG Connect, despite tight budgets and a lack of on-site production staff.
items.action: I met with our executive leadership team and presented the negative feedback from the previous event to show the real impact on community morale. When they initially said no due to costs, I offered a low-budget compromise: I proposed using affordable, basic recording gear and training community volunteers to run it. When a real-time live stream was still rejected, I pivoted and pitched saving the recordings for an on-demand archive for members, with a gradual release to the wider public later on.
items.result: While I didn't get immediate approval to roll out cameras for that specific event, the effort changed how leadership viewed community accessibility. They agreed to integrate low-cost volunteer recording workflows into the planning pipeline for all future large-scale events, ensuring long-term community feedback was taken seriously.
items.metrics: Secured executive commitment to include community-led video capture in future event frameworks.

items.title: Saving a Live Conference Track with Hackathon Teams
items.subtitle: Pivoting on stage when a niche speaker went missing in Barcelona
items.situation: The day after running a massive overnight hackathon at VMware Explore Barcelona, I was hosting a full day of live presentations in the community theater. Right as a session on a niche technical topic was supposed to start, the scheduled speaker vanished.
items.task: I had an auditorium full of people waiting for a presentation. I had to think on my feet to fill a 30-minute block on a highly specific topic without any backup slides or replacement speakers lined up.
items.action: I noticed a group of tired hackathon participants hanging out right next to the stage. I ran over, explained the situation, and asked if they would jump on stage to present their project build to the crowd. They agreed. To give them a minute to get ready and keep the audience energized, I grabbed a box of leftover hackathon t-shirts, stepped up to the microphone, and started tossing gear into the crowd while taking size requests.
items.result: The impromptu presentation ended up being a massive crowd favorite. Instead of sitting through a dry slide deck, the audience got an authentic, raw look at a real project build. We kept the track running seamlessly, turned a potential scheduling disaster into a highlight, and drove massive interest into next year's hackathon.
items.metrics: Maintained live event schedule continuity with zero open stage downtime.

items.title: Improving Team Visibility and Onboarding
items.subtitle: Setting up structured weekly syncs to keep projects from slipping
items.situation: I noticed that surrounding teams were struggling to stay on the same page. Tasks were slipping through the cracks, project updates were getting missed, and we were constantly playing catch-up instead of working ahead. On top of that, we had a new hire, Chay, who needed to be onboarded smoothly.
items.task: I wanted to fix our communication habits, streamline our daily project management, and make sure our new team member felt supported from day one.
items.action: I launched a structured weekly sync meeting for our group. Instead of just reading off a list of tasks, I designed the agenda to review active Jira tickets, call out upcoming project blockers, and identify what processes needed better documentation. I used part of the time to do individual check-ins on career goals and spent extra time walking Chay through our operational systems and introducing them to key cross-functional partners across the office.
items.result: The team's overall alignment turned around quickly. Everyone gained clear visibility into each other's weekly workloads, which stopped duplicate work and caught missed deadlines before they happened. Chay got up to speed ahead of schedule with clear performance goals, creating a much more organized and supportive team environment.
items.metrics: Onboarded new personnel ahead of schedule while standardizing project tracking workflows.

items.title: Scaling a Major Tech Hackathon from Scratch
items.subtitle: Overcoming zero lab budgets by creating a repeatable framework
items.situation: When I took over the hackathon at VMware Explore, I inherited a tough setup: we had no budget for prizes, strict new corporate compliance rules, zero dedicated lab or cloud testing environments, and no existing playbook on how to run it.
items.task: I had to design and execute a highly technical event for hundreds of developers with minimal corporate backing, while making sure the model could scale to multiple global regions.
items.action: I tracked down past participants and judges to map out what a great hackathon looked like to them. Lacking corporate cloud funding, I changed the rules to a "bring-your-own-device" structure so developers could build locally using their preferred setups. I used our event platform, RAINFocus, to scan registrations and invite developers directly. The first year was small, but I used it to cultivate a core group of passionate organizers. By year two, I used their feedback to streamline our marketing, lock down judges months in advance, and expand the model to local VMUG chapters.
items.result: The hackathon became a cornerstone track at VMware Explore in both Las Vegas and Barcelona, drawing a massive mix of registered teams and live walk-ins. It provided a completely repeatable framework that local VMUG chapters now use to run their own micro-hackathons worldwide, driving continuous engagement throughout the year.
items.metrics: Scaled a zero-budget event into a repeatable global framework across 2 major international conferences.

items.title: Maintaining Community Stability Through Corporate Layoffs
items.subtitle: Sourcing internal content creators during the Broadcom acquisition
items.situation: The 2024 Broadcom acquisition of VMware caused massive internal disruption. Amid heavy team layoffs, changing priorities, and platform consolidations, I was constantly pulled away to handle basic maintenance and fill operational gaps. Despite the internal chaos, our external community still expected regular educational resources and consistency.
items.task: I needed to deliver a reliable stream of technical content to keep the program stable, even with diminished resources and no budget for external talent.
items.action: Since I couldn't lean on traditional resources, I spent hours combing through internal company chat channels to see what our internal engineers were building. Whenever I saw someone discussing a practical workaround or a new feature, I reached out directly and convinced them to present their findings in a virtual meetup. I handled all the scheduling, promotion, and video logistics to make it as easy as possible for them to participate.
items.result: We produced 4 high-value technical deep-dive videos outside of our standard conference content, pulling in over 1,200 views. It gave our members the technical training they needed, kept our core program goals alive during a major corporate transition, and built invaluable partnerships with internal engineering teams.
items.metrics: Produced 4 technical training videos generating 1,200+ views during a major corporate restructuring.
